title: St Benedict's Church, Lincoln

infobox.value: Now the Lincoln Street Pastors Base & Office, and Unicorn Tree Bookshop.

is an ancient church in [Lincoln](/wiki/Lincoln,_England "Lincoln, England"), England which is mentioned in 1107 and before the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War") was the Lincoln civic church. It was extensively destroyed in the Civil War, and was only partially restored. The tower was re-built imitating the other late Anglo-Saxon towers in Lincoln. All that survives is the present [nave](/wiki/Nave "Nave"), which was the chancel of the former church and the chapel to the north built by Robert Tattershall in 1378.
Hill, (Sir) J.W. F, (1956), *Tudor and Stuart Lincoln*, Cambridge University Press, 57-58
Mills D.R. and Wheeler R.C. (2004), *Historic Town Plans of Lincoln*, 1610-1920. 1842/8

**St Benedict's Church, Lincoln** is an ancient church in [Lincoln](/wiki/Lincoln,_England "Lincoln, England"), England which is mentioned in 1107 and before the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War") was the Lincoln civic church. It was extensively destroyed in the Civil War, and was only partially restored. The tower was re-built imitating the other late Anglo-Saxon towers in Lincoln. All that survives is the present [nave](/wiki/Nave "Nave"), which was the chancel of the former church and the chapel to the north built by Robert Tattershall in 1378.

The church's bell known as *Old Kate*, was cast in 1585 and paid for by the Lincoln [barber surgeons](/wiki/Barber_surgeon "Barber surgeon") company. The church was closed in 1931 and demolition was proposed. However, following a public outcry, the church was saved as the result of an appeal and renovation work undertaken. It was the headquarters of the Lincoln Diocese [Mothers' Union](/wiki/Mothers%27_Union "Mothers' Union") but closed in 2018.

In June 2020 it became the base for Lincoln Street Pastors [[1]](https://streetpastors.org/locations/lincoln/) who go out on Saturday and Friday nights to listen, help and care for all those out in the City of Lincoln during the night time economy hours. In April 2022 Unicorn Tree Books moved in to become the Bookshop in St Benedict's when they had to relocate from Lincoln Central Market due to its closure.

## Parish of St Benedict

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Benedict%27s_Church,_Lincoln&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Parish of St Benedict")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/St_Benedicts_Lincoln_01.jpg/250px-St_Benedicts_Lincoln_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Benedicts_Lincoln_01.jpg)

Memorial to [William Adams Nicholson](/wiki/William_Adams_Nicholson "William Adams Nicholson"), 1853

The [parish](/wiki/Parish "Parish") was one of the smaller parishes in Lincoln. In the general re-organisation of the Lincoln parishes which took place in 1553, part of the parish of St John's was amalgamated with St Benedict's parish.[[1]](#cite_note-1) On Padley's large scale map of 1842, the parish is shown as extending from the [Brayford Pool](/wiki/Brayford_Pool "Brayford Pool") on the west to Sincil Street on the east. It included the length of the [High Street](/wiki/High_Street,_Lincoln "High Street, Lincoln") from just north of the [River Witham](/wiki/River_Witham "River Witham") and the [High Bridge](/wiki/High_Bridge,_Lincoln "High Bridge, Lincoln") to the Cornhill to the south.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

### Furnishings in the church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Benedict%27s_Church,_Lincoln&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Furnishings in the church")]

These include a late 17th-century octagonal panelled oak pulpit and an 18th-century eggcup font with swags and square stem. A royal coat of arms of 1734, was repainted in 1819, with the churchwardens' names. A brass with weepers, 1687, scrolled [cartouche](/wiki/Cartouche "Cartouche") with arms and cherubs, 1739, and a memorial to the noted Lincoln architect [William Adams Nicholson](/wiki/William_Adams_Nicholson "William Adams Nicholson") of 1853.[[3]](#cite_note-3)
